title: Where BMS Implementations Actually Break
excerpt: The block diagram is clean. The deployed system is not. Here are the specific failure modes, bad design decisions, and algorithm gaps that show up in real commercial BMS implementations — with the numbers to prove it.
pullquote: The most dangerous BMS failure mode is not the one that trips the contactor — it is the one that silently accumulates SOC error until the battery cuts power without warning at 20% displayed charge.

references.title: Waag, W. et al. (2014) — Critical review of the methods for monitoring of lithium-ion batteries, Journal of Power Sources
Andrea, D. (2010) — Battery Management Systems for Large Lithium-Ion Battery Packs, Artech House
Vetter, J. et al. (2005) — Ageing mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries, Journal of Power Sources
Battery University — BU-804: How to Prolong Lithium-Based Batteries

faqs.question: What is the most common BMS failure mode in Indian commercial EVs?
faqs.answer: Based on field reports and warranty analysis from Indian commercial EV fleets, the most common failure mode is SOC over-reading due to capacity fade neglect — the BMS calculates SOC against the original rated capacity, but the battery has degraded to 80–85% of original. This causes the vehicle to indicate 15–20% charge when the battery is actually empty, leading to unexpected power cuts. The fix is dual estimation (tracking actual capacity) or periodic forced full-cycle calibration. Neither is standard practice in most Indian commercial EV BMS designs.

faqs.question: How do temperature sensor failures manifest in BMS behaviour?
faqs.answer: An open-circuit temperature sensor (broken connection) typically reads as the ADC's rail voltage — the BMS sees an impossible temperature like -40°C or +150°C, triggers an immediate fault, and opens the contactors. This is a safe failure. A degraded sensor with high contact resistance reads a temperature lower than actual — the BMS thinks the pack is cooler than it is, allows faster charging than is safe, and does not trigger thermal derating when needed. This is an unsafe failure mode that is much harder to detect and can cause accelerated degradation or thermal events.

faqs.question: What is the pre-charge relay and what happens when it fails?
faqs.answer: The pre-charge relay and resistor form a circuit that limits inrush current when the high-voltage bus capacitance (inverter DC bus capacitors) first connects to the battery. Without pre-charge, the capacitors charge instantaneously, drawing thousands of amps for microseconds — welding the main contactor contacts. The pre-charge relay and resistor slow this process to ~100–500 ms. A common failure mode is the pre-charge relay sticking closed — the BMS thinks pre-charge completed and opens the relay, but if the pre-charge relay is welded, the main contactor may still see inrush on the next cycle. BMS firmware must verify pre-charge completion by checking bus voltage rise rate and timing.

faqs.question: Why do BMS designs fail in vehicles with poor CAN bus quality?
faqs.answer: BMS firmware typically assumes reliable CAN communication with the vehicle controller (VCU) and charger. In Indian commercial vehicles with long wiring harnesses, poor termination, and high EMI environments, CAN messages are frequently delayed, corrupted, or lost. A BMS that treats a missing VCU heartbeat as a fault and opens the contactors causes unexpected power cuts. A BMS that continues operating without VCU oversight removes the safety coordination that prevents charging at unsafe temperatures or discharging beyond safe limits. Both failure modes are common, and BMS firmware timeout handling and fallback states are frequently inadequately specified for Indian vehicle electrical environments.

faqs.question: What is contactor welding and how does the BMS detect it?
faqs.answer: Contactor welding occurs when the contacts fuse together under high fault current, preventing the relay from opening. A welded main contactor means the battery cannot be safely isolated from the vehicle. BMS contactor welding detection monitors the main bus voltage when the contactors should be open — if voltage is still present after the open command, one or both contactors are welded. Detection requires a voltage measurement point on the battery-side of the contactors, separate from the vehicle-side. Many cost-optimised BMS designs omit this measurement, making welding detection impossible and creating a latent safety risk.
